core_takeaway: The first position did not beat the last position. Agent_1 used the first turn to anchor Pencil, but the successful allocation was a mutually compatible top-item split where Agent_1 kept Pencil, Agent_2 received Jewel+Apple+Quill, and Agent_3 received Stone; all agents ended with utility 100.
why_or_counterexample: Counterexample to first-position advantage: Agent_1 spoke first and kept its demanded Pencil, but Agent_3, the last initial speaker, also secured its only valued item, Stone. Proposals that excluded Agent_3 from Stone were rejected even when they preserved Agent_1's anchor, while the accepted allocation matched all agents' top priorities.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with a strong Pencil anchor, but Agent_3's later three-way allocation framed the path that everyone endorsed: Pencil to Agent_1, Jewel+Apple+Quill to Agent_2, and Stone to Agent_3.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries converged on the same top-item logic and treated a ledger or sequencing commitment as the way to avoid renegotiation, not as a special benefit for the first speaker.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_2's formal proposal was the only clean implementation of the three-way split; Agent_1 and Agent_3 submitted variants that wrongly placed Stone with Agent_2.
phase_notes.private_voting: Every agent accepted Proposal 2 and rejected the Stone-to-Agent_2 variants, showing the final choice was driven by complete top-item satisfaction rather than first-mover control.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored but did not extract extra value
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's opening position successfully made Pencil a fixed point, but Pencil was only valuable to Agent_1 and did not prevent later speakers from getting their own core items.

evidence.quote: Pencil stays with me in Round 1

mechanisms.theme: Last initial speaker supplied the coalition template
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3's public proposal created the allocative logic that later became the accepted outcome, including Stone for the last-position agent.

mechanisms.theme: Winning proposal satisfied every top priority
mechanisms.explanation: The accepted allocation was not a privileged first-speaker allocation; it gave each agent the item or bundle that generated its maximum utility.

mechanisms.theme: Voting rejected allocations that harmed the last-position agent
mechanisms.explanation: Private votes show that preserving Agent_1's Pencil was insufficient when Stone was assigned away from Agent_3; the group selected the allocation that included Agent_3's valued item.

evidence.quote: giving me zero utility this round.
exactly my preferred outcome (Stone to me)
